data.title: Switzerland Delays Automatic Crypto Account Reporting to Foreign Tax Authorities Until 2027 Giving Banks and Investors Extra Preparation Time
data.description:
Switzerland Delays Automatic Crypto Account Reporting to Overseas Tax Authorities Until 2027

Swiss authorities have postponed the automatic exchange of crypto account information with foreign tax agencies, pushing the timeline to at least 2027.

The Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF), part of a global initiative to curb tax evasion via cryptocurrency, will still be codified into law on 1 January, but its implementation has been delayed.

🇨🇭 Switzerland delays crypto tax info sharing until 2027 📊

Switzerland will adopt the OECD’s Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) into law in Jan 2026, but won’t start sharing crypto account data with foreign tax agencies until at least 2027.

The delay comes as the… pic.twitter.com/mJ9umwKqWv

— Degen Station (@Deg3nstation) November 28, 2025

Why The Delay Happened and What Officials Say

The Swiss Federal Council and the State Secretariat for International Finance explained that the country’s tax committee “suspended deliberations on the partner states with which Switzerland intends to exchange data in accordance with the CARF.”

The delay allows authorities to finalise which international partners will receive crypto account data and ensures that technical and legal frameworks are properly established.

Officials emphasised that this postponement does not weaken Switzerland’s commitment to transparency.

A Federal Tax Administration spokesperson said,

“The delay gives them time to implement accurate and reliable reporting systems that meet international standards.”

Impact on Banks, Crypto Platforms and Investors

Under the revised schedule, Swiss banks and financial institutions holding cryptocurrency accounts will not need to report balances, transactions, or other holdings to foreign authorities until 2027.

The move provides additional time for banks and crypto platforms to adjust operations, coordinate with regulators, and ensure secure data-sharing processes.

For investors, the delay offers a temporary reprieve in managing their tax affairs.

Crypto holders in Switzerland can now take extra time to organise records and ensure compliance with domestic tax obligations.

Some analysts suggest the postponement may make Switzerland a more attractive environment for crypto activity in the short term, while also highlighting the complexities of aligning with international reporting standards.

Switzerland’s Position in Global Crypto Reporting

Switzerland is one of 75 countries that have signed on to implement CARF within the next few years, according to OECD documents.

Source: OECD

CARF was approved by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development in 2022 as part of a broader effort to exchange cryptocurrency account information internationally.

Countries such as Argentina, El Salvador, Vietnam, and India have yet to sign on.

The Swiss government has also introduced amendments to local crypto tax reporting laws and transitional provisions aimed at easing compliance for domestic crypto firms.

Earlier in 2025, the Federal Council proposed adopting CARF rules in January 2026, with the first exchange of data originally expected in 2027.

2/5 📥CARF requires reporting crypto-asset service providers - such as exchanges, brokers, and payment processors - to collect and share detailed user and transaction data with tax authorities. Transparency becomes the new norm.

— Aurum (@Aurum_Law) November 24, 2025

With the recent announcement, the precise date of the first information exchange is now uncertain.

International Implications and Coordination

Globally, other jurisdictions are taking steps to align with CARF.

Brazil is reportedly considering a tax on international crypto transfers, while the US White House has reviewed the Internal Revenue Service’s proposal to join CARF, seeking tighter reporting of capital gains from foreign exchanges.

🚨Very big news: the U.S. Treasury Department has officially sent the CARF regulations to the White House for review.

CARF, the Crypto Asset Reporting Framework, is a lengthy, international standard developed by the OECD. Nearly 90 countries have already signed on, agreeing to…

— Clinton Donnelly (@CryptoTaxFixer) November 26, 2025

Switzerland’s delay highlights the challenges regulators face in keeping pace with the rapidly evolving digital asset landscape.

Banks, platforms, and investors are urged to monitor developments closely and consult tax professionals to ensure compliance while taking advantage of the extra preparation time.

Preparing for a Smooth Transition

Swiss authorities describe the postponement as an opportunity to strengthen compliance systems and reduce operational risks.

By giving banks, crypto platforms, and investors additional time, Switzerland aims to implement a robust, reliable reporting framework that can integrate with international partners while maintaining domestic stability in the crypto market.

data.title: AB Charitable Foundation donates HK$10 million to embody the "Lion Rock Spirit".
data.description: On the afternoon of November 26, 2025, a level-5 fire broke out at Hung Fook Court in Tai Po, New Territories, Hong Kong, causing significant casualties and property damage. This severe disaster touched the hearts of the entire Hong Kong and global Chinese communities, prompting urgent action from all sectors to support affected residents through donations, supplies, and volunteer services. Against this backdrop, the AB Charitable Foundation donated HK$10 million to the Hong Kong General Association for the Promotion of Peaceful Reunification of China to jointly participate in the fire relief and post-disaster community reconstruction work at Hung Fook Court, Tai Po, and to jointly promote the sustainable development of humanitarian aid and related public undertakings. The AB Charitable Foundation was established in Ireland in May 2025, dedicated to promoting global public welfare, with a particular focus on vulnerable groups affected by war and natural disasters, especially women and children. This donation not only reflects the AB Charitable Foundation's core mission of "Tech for Good" but also emphasizes building a truly united and mutually supportive community with a shared future for mankind, transcending regional, cultural, and ethnic boundaries, considering the planet as a whole. About the AB Charitable Foundation The AB Charitable Foundation is an independent international non-governmental organization registered in Ireland and holding EU legal person status. Its technical and financial support comes from AB DAO. The Foundation is committed to using cutting-edge technologies such as blockchain and artificial intelligence to build a transparent, trustworthy, and traceable philanthropic infrastructure, thereby promoting the sustainable development of public services such as education, healthcare, environment, and humanitarian aid. AB Charitable Foundation Website: https://www.abfdn.org AB DAO Website: https://www.ab.org

data.title: Sony’s Blockchain Soneium Powers IRC APP, Giving Fans Transparent On-Chain Influence Over Japan’s Largest Idol Festival
data.description:
Sony’s Soneium Partners With IRC APP to Transform Fan Engagement in Japan

Fans of Japanese idols and fashion are stepping into a new era of recognition and reward as Soneium, Sony Block Solutions Labs’ Layer-2 blockchain platform, joins forces with IRC APP, the official app for the Idol Runway Collection (IRC).

This collaboration integrates IRC into Soneium’s AI-driven IPFi infrastructure, allowing fans’ social media activity to be tracked, scored, and rewarded directly on-chain, creating a measurable and tangible way to contribute to their favourite artists.

How AI and Blockchain Redefine Fandom

The IRC APP evaluates positive, consistent, and supportive posts on platforms such as X (formerly Twitter).

The AI calculates an “IRC Score,” which is automatically credited to users’ on-chain wallets without gas fees.

This score determines Membership rank—Regular, Bronze, Silver, or Gold—with higher tiers offering real-world benefits at IRC 2026 on 15 March 2026 in Tokyo.

6/

How IRC Score works:

AI SNS Trends evaluates your public posts on X based on:

⬛️ Positivity toward your idols
⬛️ Consistency of support
⬛️ Community contribution

Your genuine fandom activity is now recorded and comes back to you as IRC Score.

All claimed through gasless…

— Soneium 💿 (@soneium) November 28, 2025

Fans can gain early ticket access, priority entry, premium venue invitations, and influence event decisions through on-chain voting, making their participation more than just a digital gesture.

Scaling Japan’s Largest Idol and Fashion Festival

IRC, hosted by YOAKE Entertainment and supported by Tokyo Girls Collection (TGC), has grown into one of Japan’s largest idol and fashion hybrid events.

The 2025 edition drew approximately 11,800 attendees and featured 107 idol groups on a single stage.

IRC 2026 will continue this growth, uniting top acts like Nogizaka46, FRUITS ZIPPER, CANDY TUNE, CUTIE STREET, and Takamine no Nadeshiko.

「Soneium」、大型アイドルイベントの公式アプリと連携──ファンの「推し活」支援

「大型アリーナで開催される新時代のガールズイベントとして注目を集めている。今年3月に開催された際は100人を超えるアイドルが出演し、1万人以上が来場した。」https://t.co/w4pYS9mUad

— 渡辺創太 @スターテイル (@Sota_Web3) November 28, 2025

With TGC’s backing, IRC has developed a production infrastructure capable of handling millions of fans through live events, regional tours, and nationwide livestreams.

Soneium’s Layer-2 Blockchain Powers Participation

Sony launched Soneium’s mainnet on 14 January 2025 to provide a production-grade blockchain tailored for entertainment, gaming, and intellectual property applications.

Built on Ethereum’s OP Stack, the network combines high throughput with low fees, supporting large-scale applications like IRC APP.

GM. @Soneium Mainnet is live.@Sony Block Solutions Labs has launched its creator-first blockchain, Soneium.

Soneium is built on the OP Stack, and is part of the Superchain. pic.twitter.com/G0jA4e1bR0

— Optimism (@Optimism) January 14, 2025

By leveraging Account Abstraction (AA) technology, users can interact with blockchain without needing cryptocurrency knowledge or worrying about transaction costs.

The platform ensures that fan contributions are permanently recorded, immutable, and portable across events and services, turning everyday social media activity into a form of digital asset.

Lessons From Asia Show Web3 Participation Drives Value

Soneium’s approach reflects lessons learned from prior Asian entertainment experiments.

Korea’s girl group tripleS, for instance, integrated blockchain to give fans voting power via NFTs and tokens in the COSMO app.

This model generated revenue even before the group debuted, offering fair compensation and transparent fan participation.

In contrast, platforms like Momentrica by Dunamu and HYBE failed because they treated NFTs as static collectibles without ongoing utility or engagement, resulting in an operating loss of 13 billion KRW ($8.88 million).

The difference is clear: successful Web3 entertainment requires participatory structures rather than just digital merchandise.

Fan Scores Influence Real-World Access and Decisions

Within IRC APP, accumulated scores not only elevate membership ranks but also grant access to exclusive event benefits.

Voting occurs on-chain, preventing manipulation or hidden adjustments common in traditional fan polls.

By publishing voting history and tallying results transparently, the system provides verifiable influence over aspects of IRC 2026, creating a model of fandom where contributions carry both symbolic and practical value.

IRC APP Gains Backing From Japan’s Leading Entertainment Companies

YOAKE Entertainment, winner of Sony’s Soneium Spark startup contest, operates IRC APP with investment support from major Japanese entertainment firms including TWIN PLANET, Asobi System, W TOKYO, and Y&N Brothers.

Say hello to @YOAKEofficialEN’s new IRC APP, a fandom platform in partnership with @idolrunwaycolle and @TGCnews, which is set to be the leading hub for onchain entertainment.

Built on @soneium and powered by @record_protocol, the IRC APP will redefine how IPFi unlocks real… https://t.co/TigG1eaZnd

— Startale 💿 (@StartaleGroup) November 28, 2025

The collaboration highlights the growing trend of combining idols, fashion, and technology to engage fans in measurable ways, positioning IRC as a blueprint for digital fandom in Japan and beyond.

Will the Model Expand Beyond Idol Culture

With its scalable architecture, Soneium is not limiting the IRC APP to idol fandom.

The same participation-driven framework could be applied to anime, fashion, and other creative industries, enabling fans worldwide to have a measurable, rewarded role in shaping cultural content.

data.title: ALT5 Sigma Turmoil Deepens as Trump-Linked WLFI Project Faces Legal and Political Fire
data.description:
Leadership Shakeup Follows $1.5B WLFI Token Deal and Mounting Internal Pressure

ALT5 Sigma, a crypto treasury company tied to World Liberty Financial (WLFI) — a token project linked to the Trump family — has announced the firing of two of its top executives amid growing scrutiny surrounding both the firm and its political associations.

Trump Family-Linked Alt5 Sigma Ousts Top Execs After CEO Suspension Shakes Up Leadership

The company is on its third CEO in six weeks, with Tony Isaac appointed as Acting CEO, and has named Steven Plumb as its new CFO. pic.twitter.com/rVC2eetrBB

— SmartChainLab🧠 (@haruna_muh17218) November 28, 2025

The Nevada-based company confirmed in recent filings that CEO Jonathan Hugh and chief operating officer Ron Pitters departed in November as part of a rapid leadership overhaul, with both exits described as “without cause.” with no additional given for their termination.

Tony Isaac, the firm’s president and long-time board director, has stepped in as acting CEO while ALT5 finalizes Hugh’s departure terms. The shakeup comes just months after ALT5 committed $1.5 billion to purchase WLFI tokens as the cornerstone of its new crypto treasury strategy, placing the company at the center of one of the most politically sensitive blockchain initiatives in the United States.

Eric Trump, son of President Donald Trump, briefly served as a director before transitioning into a board observer role to comply with Nasdaq listing guidelines. The company’s alignment with WLFI has attracted intense attention from Democratic lawmakers, who argue that the Trump family’s involvement in token issuance raises conflict-of-interest and national-security concerns, particularly as WLFI markets itself as a decentralized finance platform while maintaining deeply political ties.

This move comes after one of ALT5's shareholders, Jon Isaac, was rumoured to have been under investigation by the SEC for earning inflation and insider sales.

World Liberty Financial later distanced itself from Jon, saying he has no knowledge of the investigation

"For the record: Jon Isaac is not, and never was the president of ALT5 Sigma, and he is not an advisor to the company. The company has no knowledge of any currency investigation regarding its activities by the US SEC."

WLFI’s token price has meanwhile declined amid the heightened scrutiny, underscoring the market’s growing anxiety over both regulatory pressure and corporate instability.

Legal Clouds Around WLFI Intensify as Lawmakers Press for Investigation

Behind the corporate reshuffling lies a deeper set of legal challenges tied to WLFI and entities associated with the Trump family. A subsidiary connected to World Liberty Financial was ruled criminally liable for money laundering in Rwanda earlier this year, a decision that also found an ALT5-linked principal guilty in the same case.

The company maintains that both the subsidiary and the executive have appealed the ruling, asserting they were victims of an external fraud scheme.

Even so, ALT5 has acknowledged that its board was not informed of the Rwandan case until months after the decision — a revelation that further destabilized internal governance and preceded the unexplained suspension of former CEO Peter Tassiopoulos in October.

Political pressure escalated again in November when Democratic lawmakers urged U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi to investigate allegations that WLFI had sold tokens to sanctioned entities in Russia and North Korea.

BREAKING: The Trump-owned Crypto company World Liberty Finance, has been found to have raised money from a group with ties to a notorious North Korean hacking organization, a sanctioned Russian “Ruble-backed sanctions evasion tool,” an Iranian crypto exchange and Tornado Cash, a… pic.twitter.com/ienk4Gd5mG

— Brian Krassenstein (@krassenstein) November 18, 2025

Legislators warned that the Trump family’s crypto ventures — which they claim have generated over $1 billion in profits — could create avenues for illicit financial activity and allow foreign actors to purchase political access.

The structure of ALT5’s agreement with WLFI has also drawn attention, as a Trump-affiliated entity stands to receive 75% of all token sale proceeds, a revenue share that could exceed $500 million if WLFI adoption accelerates.

Despite the mounting concerns, World Liberty Financial has downplayed the leadership upheaval and regulatory pressure, with spokesperson David Wachsman saying that “the filing speaks for itself” and expressing continued confidence in ALT5’s future direction.

Yet the combination of executive turnover, legal exposure, and political inquiry has placed both companies under an increasingly harsh spotlight, raising questions about governance, transparency and the broader implications of intertwining high-profile political families with emerging crypto-financial systems.

data.title: The HTTPS Moment for Ethereum Privacy
data.description:
Author: Zhixiong Pan Source: chainfeeds

This Ethereum Privacy Stack event, jointly organized by the Privacy & Scaling Explorations (PSE) team, Web3Privacy Now, and core members of the Ethereum Foundation (EF), is one of the highest-profile vertical events during Devconnect ARG 2025. The event brings together Vitalik Buterin, the founder of Tor, core EF researchers, founders of privacy protocols (Railgun, 0xbow, Aztec, etc.), and leading legal experts. Its core purpose is to reshape the Ethereum privacy ecosystem at a turning point of increasing regulatory pressure and maturing technology, bridging technological silos and setting the tone for a privacy roadmap for the next 3-5 years.

The Ethereum Privacy Stack, held during Devconnect Buenos Aires 2025, is the most important privacy-themed gathering in the Ethereum ecosystem this year.

The most significant consensus reached at this event was the establishment of the concept of "Holistic Privacy": privacy is no longer merely a collection of on-chain tools such as zero-knowledge proofs (ZK) or mixers, but a complete closed loop spanning the network transport layer (Tor), RPC reading layer, data storage layer, and user interaction front-end. As emphasized by Vitalik Buterin and Tor project founder Roger Dingledine, if the underlying network leaks IP addresses, anonymity at the application layer is meaningless. The community has reached a consensus that Ethereum must adhere to the "barrel theory," patching the weakest link—metadata leakage—to truly become a censorship-resistant "world ledger." Trend Insight: The Decisive Battle Towards "Default Privacy" and User Experience Participants generally agreed that Web3 privacy is undergoing a critical moment similar to the Web2 transition from HTTP to HTTPS. Privacy technology should no longer be the exclusive domain of "geeks" or "hackers," nor should it bear the moral burden of "hiding crime." Through comparisons of Railgun, Kohaku wallets, and historical Web2 experiences, speakers pointed out that the next key step is to "stigmatize non-privacy behavior," that is, to treat transparent transactions as an aberration akin to running naked on the internet. By 2026, the Ethereum community aims to reduce the cost of privacy-focused transactions to an acceptable level (e.g., only twice the cost of ordinary transactions) and achieve a seamless, one-click experience, thus opening doors not only for retail investors but also for traditional financial institutions that are previously unable to participate due to a lack of trade secret protection. Core Controversy: The Concern of a "Civil War" Between the Compliance Spectrum and L1 While the technology roadmap is becoming clearer, ideological tensions persist. The biggest point of contention lies in the struggle between "compliant privacy" and "permissionless privacy." One side, represented by Privacy Pools, advocates for proactively isolating illicit funds through "proof of dissociation" in exchange for regulatory tolerance and institutional adoption; the other side insists on pure cypherpunk spirit, arguing that any form of compliance compromise will ultimately lead to censorship. Furthermore, Andy Guzman of PSE warned of a potential "civil war": whether privacy features should be pushed down to the Ethereum core protocol layer (L1). Writing it to L1 could bring unified liquidity and default protection, but it could also bring huge regulatory risks and protocol complexity; this choice will determine the future political nature of Ethereum. Beyond software-level discussions, this event unusually delved into the physical and network layers. From "running your own node" to "trustless trustless execution environments (TEEs)," the community realized that if backdoors are implanted in the hardware, all upper-layer encryption will fail. Censorship resistance has been redefined as a public infrastructure similar to a "fire escape," seemingly without market demand in peacetime, but the only hope for survival in times of crisis. Whether building decentralized VPNs (such as Nym and HOPR) or using ZK-TLS for "guerrilla interoperability," the aim is to build a system that remains robust even under extreme geopolitical conflicts.

Self-Help Through Law and Culture

Faced with the plight of the Tornado Cash developers, the event was permeated with an urgent atmosphere of "self-help." Legal experts and developers unanimously called for the establishment of a strong legal defense fund and policy lobbying groups.

data.title: What is the background of the application that Vitalik is supporting? Is private communication the next big thing?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On November 27, 2025, Vitalik posted on X, strongly supporting two decentralized messaging applications, Session and SimpleX Chat, and donating 128 ETH to each.

Vitalik pointed out: Digital privacy protection in encrypted messaging is crucial. Two important directions for future development in this field are: (i) permissionless account creation; and (ii) metadata privacy protection.

Neither of these software applications is perfect; they still have a long way to go before achieving truly optimal user experience and security. Strong metadata privacy protection requires decentralization, which is inherently difficult to achieve, further complicated by user expectations of multi-device support. Defending against Sybil attacks/denial-of-service attacks on the messaging routing network and user end (without mandatory reliance on phone numbers) also adds to the difficulty. These issues require more attention. Best wishes to all teams dedicated to solving these important problems.

I. What are Session and SimpleX Chat?

1. Session

The development of Session began in 2018, initiated by the Oxen Privacy Tech Foundation, headquartered in Australia. The project was initially a fork of another instant messaging software, Signal, designed to expand upon it. However, due to concerns about the centralized structure of the Signal protocol and potential metadata collection issues, the team decided to take a different approach, creating their own protocol, the "Session Protocol." This approach prioritizes increased anonymity and decentralization.

During development, the team encountered various challenges, leading to the abandonment or modification of many features. In 2024, facing increasingly stringent privacy and surveillance legislation in Australia, the Swiss-based Session Technology Foundation was established to take over the development and release of the application. Session is a decentralized, end-to-end encrypted instant messaging tool designed to minimize metadata leaks—metadata refers to information surrounding content, such as IP addresses or sending times. Session allows account creation without phone numbers or email addresses. It uses a randomly generated 66-bit alphanumeric combination for user identification. Communication between users, including messages, voice clips, photos, and files, is end-to-end encrypted using the Session protocol. Session uses the Loki blockchain network for transmission. In 2021, an independent review by the third-party organization Quarkslab confirmed these claims. In 2025, Session announced its migration to its own network, Session Network—a decentralized, open-source blockchain network designed specifically for transmitting encrypted data in the Session instant messaging software. Session also issued a token, SESH. According to CoinGecko data, the token rose to a high of nearly $0.30 due to Vitalik X's tweets, before falling back to $0.1961 at the time of writing. 2. SimpleX Chat SimpleX Chat is also an open-source instant messaging tool that emphasizes "no user ID" – no phone number, email address, or random username/ID. It supports end-to-end encryption (E2EE) and the protocol is designed to withstand future quantum computing. According to the SimpleX Chat official white paper: The SimpleX Messaging Protocol (SMP) is a one-way message sending protocol that relies on an intermediary server. Messages are delivered through a one-way queue created by the receiver. The SMP protocol allows SMP servers to act as proxies, sending messages using "private routes" to protect the sender's transmission information (IP address and session) from eavesdropping by servers chosen (and potentially controlled) by the receiver. SMP runs on top of a transport protocol that provides integrity, server authentication, confidentiality, and transport channel binding. SimpleX servers are one such protocol. The SimpleX network refers to the collection of SimpleX servers used to support SMP. SimpleX client libraries communicate with SimpleX servers via SMP and provide a low-level API that is not typically used by applications. SimpleX Agents interact with SimpleX Clients, providing a higher-level API for application use. They are typically embedded as libraries but can also be abstracted as local services. SimpleX proxies communicate with other proxies via end-to-end encrypted envelopes provided by the SMP protocol—the syntax and semantics of messages exchanged between proxies are defined by the SMP proxy protocol. Next year, SimpleX will launch "vouchers," which users will need to purchase and donate to the community (such as the Bitcoin community) to host the necessary servers. II. Behind Vitalik's Appeal: Protecting Instant Messaging Privacy is Becoming a Necessity Under the influence of measures such as the EU's previously proposed "chat control," the privacy of instant messaging applications has been a hot topic. These measures will force platforms such as Telegram, WhatsApp, and Signal to allow authorities to review messages before encrypting and sending them. The reason Session and SimpleX Chat gained Vitalik's favor is that they precisely address his two core directions: "permissionless account creation" and "metadata privacy protection." Permissionless account creation means users don't need to provide any personal identification credentials, completely breaking the identity binding shackles of traditional communication tools and avoiding the chain of risks caused by identity information leakage. Metadata privacy protection goes beyond simple content encryption, extending protection to metadata levels such as message sending time, sender IP address, receiver address, and message size—information that may seem insignificant, but through big data analysis, it can outline a user's social relationships, behavioral habits, and even asset status, making its privacy value no less than the communication content itself. Back in April of this year, Vitalik proposed a roadmap aimed at making it easier and more natural for ordinary users to conduct private transactions and anonymous on-chain interactions without making major changes to the network's core protocols. The proposed roadmap covers four main forms of privacy: privacy of on-chain payments, partial anonymization of on-chain activities within applications, privacy of on-chain reads, and network-level anonymization. Subsequently, Ethereum has been moving towards a greater emphasis on "privacy": At the Ethereum Developers Conference from November 17th to 22nd, Vitalik released Kohaku, an Ethereum privacy-preserving encryption tool—a new privacy and security toolkit for Ethereum wallets. On November 25th, while Wall Street banks like JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, and Morgan Stanley were still grappling with the aftermath of a massive data breach, Vitalik delivered a crucial message: "Privacy is not a feature; privacy is basic hygiene." Alexander Linton, Chairman of the Session Technology Foundation, noted, "Unfortunately, regulatory and technological advancements are currently threatening the future of private instant messaging. However, the challenges facing private instant messaging are solvable, and I think Vitalik clearly understands the importance of decentralization in this fight. Everyone working in private messaging is currently facing some kind of threat due to regulations such as chat controls, but this support helps us focus on our mission." Chris McCabe, co-founder of Session, stated, "It's amazing how clearly we see that Vitalik and many others around the world understand what true privacy is and what people need to live freely." Raising global awareness of encrypted, decentralized messaging should be a key next step. "If we want to send a message to the world, it's that you don't have to be a commodity; you can be who you want to be and express yourself freely. Privacy is a right, and you only need to know it exists." Zac Williamson, co-founder of Aztec Network, an Ethereum Layer 2 network focused on privacy protection, points out: "People's lives are increasingly dependent on digital spaces. These spaces are extremely vulnerable to surveillance that is unparalleled in the real world. Privacy is crucial; it allows people to act freely in online interactions, rather than becoming digital commodities whose data is collected, sold to the highest bidder, and used to harm users' interests." III. Conclusion: Is the next big trend the "privacy" track? For ordinary users, Session and SimpleX Chat offer a new option for private communication, especially in protecting sensitive information, and their future value is already beginning to emerge. For the crypto industry, these two "explorers" provide more ideas for the complete development of Web3 privacy infrastructure. While the privacy communication sector currently lacks the grand narrative of sectors like AI and DeFi, Vitalik and the Ethereum team's focus on it may attract more projects and capital. Amidst increasing global regulation and ongoing data breach scandals, decentralized privacy communication could be the next industry hotspot as more developers enter the field.

data.title: Visa Builds New International Settlement Layer Using Stablecoins for Banks in Europe, Middle East and Africa
data.description:
Visa Expands Stablecoin Settlement Across CEMEA Through Partnership With Aquanow

Visa is bringing blockchain settlement into the heart of international finance, moving beyond consumer-facing services to tackle the complex back-end of cross-border payments.

In a strategic collaboration with digital asset infrastructure provider Aquanow, Visa is enabling banks and financial institutions across Central and Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (CEMEA) to settle transactions using approved stablecoins, including USDC.

Aquanow x Visa - Stablecoins Enter The Financial Mainstream.@Visa has selected @Aquanow to expand stablecoin settlement across CEMEA, enabling issuers and acquirers to settle with approved stablecoins like USDC.

Faster cycles. Lower friction. 365-day settlement.

Pilot →… pic.twitter.com/twyDRi4cSg

— Aquanow (@aquanow) November 26, 2025

Why Visa Targets Back-End Payments

The initiative focuses on the infrastructure that governs the speed and cost of international transfers, rather than the retail side of payments.

By leveraging blockchain-based stablecoin settlements, Visa aims to eliminate delays caused by traditional banking hours, intermediary fees, and multi-step reconciliation processes.

Godfrey Sullivan, Head of Product and Solutions for CEMEA at Visa, said,

“By harnessing the power of stablecoins and pairing them with our trusted global technology, we are enabling financial institutions in CEMEA to experience faster and simpler settlements.”

The approach allows institutions to move money internationally at any hour, including weekends and holidays, reduce settlement costs associated with foreign exchange and intermediaries, and finalise transfers directly on blockchain rails rather than relying solely on legacy networks.

Visa presents the shift as an upgrade to existing systems, rather than a replacement, building a parallel settlement structure capable of handling speed and scale the old rails cannot.

Stablecoins Are Entering Mainstream Settlement

Stablecoins are increasingly being used beyond the crypto niche as practical tools for institutional liquidity.

Deutsche Börse recently announced plans to integrate its euro-pegged stablecoin EURAU across custody and settlement services, complementing earlier collaborations with Circle’s EURC and Société Générale-Forge’s EURCV.

These moves indicate that stablecoins could gain a foothold in mainstream financial systems faster than central bank digital currencies (CBDCs).

Regulators are taking notice.

The Basel Committee is reviewing whether the current 1,250% risk weighting for crypto exposures remains relevant as stablecoins are increasingly applied in traditional settlement.

Today's Kiffmeister #Fintech Digest features the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision shelving 1,250% risk weighting on stablecoins and other permissionless blockchain-based digital assets. For more detail go to: https://t.co/vw64GCa0JJpic.twitter.com/8wDvIGrjqE

— John Kiff (@Kiffmeister) November 19, 2025

The Bank of England expects the UK to align with the U.S. on stablecoin regulation, marking a shift in the regulatory landscape this year.

Visa Builds on USDC Pilot Success

Visa’s work with stablecoins is not new.

In 2023, it piloted USDC settlement with select clients, achieving an annualised transaction volume exceeding $2.5 billion.

The current expansion with Aquanow aims to bring that capability to a wider network of issuers and acquirers across the CEMEA region.

Phil Sham, CEO of Aquanow, said,

“The partnership opens new pathways for institutions to participate in the digital economy. Visa’s reliable global network has long moved money securely and efficiently. Together, Visa and Aquanow are unlocking new ways for institutions to participate in the digital economy, leveraging stablecoin technology to settle with the speed and transparency of the internet.”

How This Changes Cross-Border Payments

Banks and fintechs have been seeking faster settlement cycles, particularly in high-volume corridors where traditional banking chains create bottlenecks.

By adopting stablecoin settlement, financial institutions can bypass slow correspondent networks and streamline operational workflows, improving liquidity and efficiency.

Visa’s expansion reflects a broader trend where blockchain-based settlement is no longer confined to cryptocurrency markets but is being used to enhance mainstream payment infrastructure.

While regulatory clarity remains a work in progress, the initiative signals growing confidence in stablecoins as viable tools for international finance.

Institutional Adoption Remains Key

The integration of Aquanow’s infrastructure with Visa’s global network allows banks, neobanks, brokerages, and payment companies to participate in faster, more transparent settlement.

With Visa reporting strong performance in Q2 2025—$9.6 billion in net revenue, up 9% year-over-year—the company continues to invest in parallel systems that prepare financial institutions for 24/7, blockchain-enabled money movement, while maintaining traditional rails for those who rely on them.

$V Visa Q2 2025 Earnings 💰

Highlights
- Q2 FY25 revenue: $9.6 billion (9% Y/Y growth, $50 million beat)
- Non-GAAP EPS: $2.76 ($0.08 beat)
- Global network expansion: 4.8 billion cards (7% Y/Y growth)
- Cross-border volume: 13% increase
- Processed transactions: 9% increase
-… pic.twitter.com/05QFjHxt0i

— Eric García (@SuEricGP) May 2, 2025

data.title: Predicting Market Supercycles
data.description:
Author: Tulip King; Source: X, @0xTulipKing; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Prediction markets are not yet fully understood, and that's your opportunity. We are witnessing a complete reshaping of how markets work, how information is priced, and most importantly—how the future is shaped. If you ignore this, you will miss the biggest trading opportunity in the market since the digitalization of options.

The iPhone Moment

In every technological revolution, there is a wonderful period when people are unable to perceive disruptive changes because they are stuck in old ways of thinking. In 2007, Nokia executives looked at the iPhone and said, "It doesn't even have a keyboard." They compared it to a mobile phone, ignoring the fact that it should be compared to a computer. The iPhone is not competing with existing mobile phones—it has revolutionized the concept of a single-function device.

This is exactly what is happening in prediction markets today.

People saw Polymarket and thought it was some strange, illiquid betting site. They compared it to DraftKings (a sports betting platform) or the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) and found it inferior. They made a Nokia-esque mistake. Polymarket isn't a better betting site—it's replacing the entire concept of a professional financial market. Consider this: when you strip away all the complexities, what is the essence of each financial instrument? Options: betting "yes" or "no" on price levels; Insurance: betting "yes" or "no" on the occurrence of a disaster; Credit Default Swaps: betting "yes" or "no" on bankruptcy; Sports Betting: betting "yes" or "no" on the outcome of a sporting event. We've built trillion-dollar industries around fundamentally binary questions, each with its own infrastructure, regulatory systems, and monopolistic intermediaries from which it extracts profits. Polymarket simplifies all of this into one fundamental element: creating markets for any observable event, allowing people to trade, and settling accounts when reality determines the outcome. It's not better than DraftKings at sports betting, nor better than CME at derivatives. It does something more fundamental—simplifying all markets to their most basic units and rebuilding them from there. Polymarket is like the iPhone; everything else is just an app. Multi-dimensional Trading When all trading takes place in the same venue, possibilities that didn't exist before are unlocked. Imagine five years ago, if you wanted to construct a position expressing the view: "I think the Fed will raise rates, but tech stocks will rise anyway because Trump will tweet some positive news about artificial intelligence." You would need accounts at different financial institutions, dealing with different regulatory frameworks, and using different leverage. And the "Trump mentions" trade simply didn't exist in the market back then. On Polymarket, this can be done with just three clicks. More importantly, these aren't three separate bets, but rather a coherent worldview expressed through interconnected positions. You could buy a short position on "the Fed pauses rate hikes," a long position on "the Nasdaq hits a record high," and a long position on "Trump will mention artificial intelligence in his next speech." This correlation itself is the key trade.

For those who are still following, here's an example. Last month on Polymarket, you could build a position like this: buy a position at 67 cents on "Hyperliquid won't airdrop before December 31st," and at the same time buy a position at 13 cents on "Will Hyperliquid fall to $20 by 2026?". Consider this outcome matrix: **Maximum Profit Scenario:** Hyperliquid doesn't airdrop, and the price drops to $20 this year. Given the current market conditions, this seems like a reasonable assumption, and the price should be 8% higher than the current market value. You are buying this outcome at a low price. **Minor Profit Scenario:** Hyperliquid airdrops, causing the price to drop to $20, or the price drops to $20 without an airdrop. These two scenarios are the most likely, with a probability of 63%. **Full Loss Scenario:** Hyperliquid airdrops, and the price remains above $20. Given the market's significant concerns about the new supply from team unlocks, it seems that airdrops introducing more supply would be met with a very negative reaction. The probability of this outcome is less than 29%, so you're selling this outcome at a high price. This sounds like hedging, but it's much more than that. It expresses a complex view of how the market handles new token supply, a view that's difficult to articulate accurately anywhere else. Traditional markets force you to compress complex ideas into rough directional bets. You might have a complex argument that Nvidia's (NVDA) earnings will exceed expectations, but it will still fall because market expectations are ahead of reality. In the options market, you can only choose between call and put options, perhaps constructing a spread that barely expresses your meaning but is costly. On the Polymarket, you only need to buy the options "Nvidia earnings exceed expectations" and "Nvidia stock price drops 5% after earnings release" to accurately express your view. The real brilliance lies in what you do when you start thinking about cross-market connections that shouldn't exist but do. For example, a hurricane is approaching Florida, while the Tampa Bay Buccaneers are playing in Detroit. Conventional wisdom considers these two events unrelated. But you have an assumption: if the hurricane does hit Florida, the NFL referees will likely favor the Buccaneers, creating a compelling narrative. So, you bet 60 cents on the Buccaneers losing and 20 cents on the hurricane hitting Florida. You're not betting on a particular outcome, but on a connection structure. You profit from understanding how narratives influence referee decisions. This is what I mean when I say prediction markets don't compete with existing markets—they operate on a completely different level of abstraction. All other markets offer only one option, while a polymarket offers an infinite number of options, and more importantly, it allows you to choose specific combinations of options based on your understanding of how the world works. Why are the "savvy" people wrong (again)? The first criticism you hear about prediction markets is always about liquidity. "Oh, you can't trade at scale." "The spreads are too big." "It's just idiots gambling with their lunch money." But that's not a loophole—that's your opportunity.

Think about the reasons for the impending liquidity explosion from a mechanistic perspective. Traditional market making is relatively simple—you usually make markets on things that have a clear correlation with other things. Stock options are correlated with stock prices, futures are correlated with spot prices. Each thing has hedging, correlation, and models that can be relied upon. This is why a few companies like Citadel and Jane Street are able to make markets for thousands of financial instruments.

Predicting the market is more difficult.

Each market type requires its own specialized intelligence: Sports markets need models that can be updated with every score, every injury report, and every weather change. Political markets require natural language processing to analyze polls, speeches, and social media sentiment. Event markets need machine learning systems that can calculate basic probabilities based on historical data. Mention markets require language models trained on thousands of text records. You can't let one market maker monopolize all markets because each market requires completely different expertise. In the long run, this actually benefits liquidity. We will no longer see a few giants monopolizing all market-making businesses, but rather an explosive growth in professional market makers. Some quantitative analyst teams will become the world's best experts at pricing mentions in the market. Other teams will dominate weather-related events. Still others will focus on celebrity behavior. This seemingly weak fragmentation will actually create resilience and depth. In the next five years, we will see a completely new type of financial company emerge—prediction market experts. They won't be making markets in the stock or bond markets, but in reality itself. And the companies that first achieve large-scale success in this will become the bastions of the new financial system. The Disturbing Facts About Truth Now let's put forward some real arguments: prediction markets aren't about prediction itself; they're about creating a financial incentive for truth. We live in a bizarre age where everyone has an opinion on everything, but nobody is really taking risks. Your favorite Twitter analyst has predicted twelve recessions that have only occurred twice in the past. CNBC commentators have botched more accounts than Do Kwon. Yet they retain their platforms, their audiences, and continue making incorrect predictions without consequence. This model is unsustainable, and everyone knows that deep down. The information ecosystem we've built rewards engagement, not accuracy; volume matters more than correctness. Social media has exacerbated this problem—the most popular opinions now prevail, regardless of their accuracy. Those with the most followers become experts, and so-called experts are simply those who get the most likes. Prediction markets have completely disrupted this model. Suddenly, there's a price to pay for both correct and incorrect predictions. The market doesn't care if you graduated from Harvard, have a verified account, or have written books on marketing. It only cares if your predictions are correct. When you build a system that only rewards accuracy, something amazing happens: those who predict correctly suddenly have a reason to speak, while those who predict incorrectly finally have a reason to shut up. But this is not simply a matter of transferring wealth from those who shouldn't have it to those who should. Prediction markets are building a parallel information system whose operating mechanism is drastically different from our current media ecosystem. In the old world: information spreads virally on social networks. In prediction markets: information is priced according to reality. These are fundamentally different selection mechanisms, and therefore produce drastically different results. We have been observing this phenomenon in real time on Polymarket. The predictive power of the political market has surpassed any aggregated poll data. Markets related to the Federal Reserve fluctuate before any economists update their forecasts. The yield market has already priced in results that won't appear in analyst reports for weeks. This is not because traders in the prediction market are smarter, but because their incentives are to ensure accurate predictions, not to chase hype.

Bounty Markets, Not Prediction Markets

Now things are getting really strange, and I think everyone's overlooking the most important dynamic. We keep calling these "prediction markets," but that's like calling Bitcoin "digital gold"—technically correct, but completely ignoring something more important. True prediction markets are passive observers. They price probabilities but don't influence the outcome. Weather futures markets don't change the weather. But polymarkets aren't passive, and that's the key: **any market with human participants contains the potential to change the outcome.**

Let me explain what I mean. Someone placed a bet on whether someone would throw a green object during a WNBA game, and someone calculated the odds:

  1. Buying $10,000 worth of YES at 15 cents per bet.

  2. buying $10,000 worth of YES at 15 cents per bet.

  4. Throw the item yourself.

  5. When the market result is "YES", you receive $66,000.

  6. Net profit after deducting legal fees and lifetime ban: approximately $50,000

The equilibrium dynamics here are very interesting. Theoretically, the WNBA disorderly conduct bounty should be set at exactly this price:

Compensation Amount = Criminal Charges + Social Shame + Lifetime Ban + Required Effort

If the price is too high, it will attract imitators; if the price is too low, no one will pay attention. The market will find the optimal equilibrium price.

The "prediction market" has become a bounty market—it no longer predicts whether someone will throw something, but instead provides a specific amount of money for someone to make that happen. This isn't a loophole, nor is it manipulation; it's the most important characteristic of prediction markets, yet no one mentions it.

Consider this thought experiment. I decide to run for mayor of New York City. The market gives me a fairly reasonable chance of winning, only 0.5%. Based on this probability, I could spend $100,000 to buy 20 bets, each worth $5,000. If I win, each bet would bring in a staggering $1 million.

... What's even more interesting is that I used these betting shares as rewards for my campaign team. I hired 20 hitmen and promised each of them $1 million if we ultimately won. I just created something that shouldn't exist: a market-funded political campaign where my leverage increased as the odds of winning decreased. The market was essentially saying, "This outcome is too unlikely, so we're giving you 20 to 1 odds to make it happen." Prediction became a bounty. The market wasn't just observing reality—it was also funding specific versions of the future. Some events are completely unaffected by bounty mechanisms. For example, the elected president is inherently valuable, and no bounty from any prediction market could significantly increase its appeal. But for thousands of other events—from corporate decisions to cultural phenomena to sporting events—this bounty mechanism is real and in effect. We haven't arrived at the kind of future politics envisioned by Robin Hansen, where prediction markets guide governance through wisdom. What we get is something even stranger: the market pays people to make specific future events happen. Conclusion: My prediction is that within ten years, the Polymarket model will devour most of the traditional financial system. This is not only because it's a lower-cost, better-experience trading venue, but also because it integrates all markets into a single foundational platform and rebuilds upon it, which is fundamentally far more efficient than maintaining thousands of specialized market structures. The dominoes will fall in the following order: DraftKings—Sports betting is essentially a lower-odds prediction market. CBOE—Options are essentially complex binary bets on price levels. Insurance—merely a prediction market, where only one party can trade. Credit Markets—bankruptcy prediction (with additional steps) Every vertical will resist, will regulate, but will eventually yield because they will realize they are not competing with better products—but with better principles. What we are truly witnessing now is a complete restructuring of how markets operate. You no longer bet on prices, interest rates, or volatility, but on events, the correlations between events, and most importantly, the probability of changing events. Savvy investors should prepare for this now. This is not just about buying tokens or trading markets, but about thinking about what will happen when any observable event can have an active market. What will happen when every corporate decision has a prediction market? What will happen when every piece of legislation has odds? What happens when every cultural trend has a price? What happens when entrepreneurs can raise funds by selling shares of their seemingly impossible ventures? What happens when anyone with confidence in the future can leverage that confidence? We're not just building better markets; we're building a mechanism that incentivizes people to create the future.

data.title: Do Kwon Faces 40-Year Threat in South Korea as Defense Seeks 5-Year Cap in U.S.
data.description:
Do Kwon Pleads For His Prison Term To Be Capped At Five Years

Terraform Labs co-founder Do Kwon is fighting a legal battle on two fronts as he is begging for U.S. prosecutors to limit his prison term to five years as prosecutors in South Korea are weighing a potential 40-year sentence over his role in the collapse of the Terra ecosystem.

His legal team argues that extending his U.S. punishment beyond five years would be excessive, given the harsh punishment that is awaiting him at home.

🚨 LATEST: Do Kwon has asked a U.S. judge to limit his sentence to five years for the Terra collapse, even as he faces up to 40 years in a separate case in South Korea. pic.twitter.com/COdpaX9o8u

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) November 27, 2025

Kwon is urging the Manhattan federal court to adopt a five-year sentence, which he frames as proportionate given his nearly three years behind bars — including what his lawyers describe as “severe, punitive conditions” in Montenegro.

The defense argues that a sentence longer than five years would be “far greater than necessary,” especially since he still faces potential decades-long punishment in South Korea.

Under Kwon’s plea agreement, U.S. prosecutors committed not to seek more than 12 years. However, the presiding judge retains discretion to impose up to 25 years, leaving the final outcome uncertain.

Following his extradition from Montenegro, Kwon pleaded guilty in August to two counts of wire fraud and conspiracy to defraud in connection with Terra’s catastrophic $40 billion collapse in 2022. His team highlights that he has already endured significant personal and financial consequences, noting the years he has spent in custody and the global reputational fallout.

Do Kwon arrested in Montenegro.

What did he think he was a Bond villain? This place is too nice to hide out. pic.twitter.com/IoFSm1ojio

— HORSE 🏴‍☠️ (@TheFlowHorse) March 23, 2023

The plea deal also requires Kwon to forfeit more than $19 million in assets, including multiple properties — adding a heavy financial layer to the criminal penalties.

South Korean Prosecution: A Potential 40-Year Sentence Still Awaits

Even after the U.S. case concludes, Kwon’s legal problems will intensify. South Korean prosecutors are preparing their own criminal sentencing recommendation, reportedly eyeing up to 40 years in prison for his role in the Terra collapse — a punishment reflecting the scale of losses to local retail investors and the broader impact on the country’s crypto markets.

So $LUNA Do Kwon is asking a US judge to lower his prison term to 5 YEARS, while South Korean prosecutors are pursuing up to 40 YEARS in a separate case.

I’d rather see all his wallets and TFL’s $LUNC wallets burned. Let’s see that. 💎🤲🏻 #Cryptopic.twitter.com/a3pxjftvuc

— Mr. Diamondhandz1💎 (@MrDiamondhandz1) November 28, 2025

This looming prosecution is central to Kwon’s argument for leniency in the U.S., with his defense stressing that any lengthy American sentence would stack atop a potentially decades-long term in Seoul, compounding his total time behind bars.

U.S. District Judge Paul Engelmayer is scheduled to sentence Kwon on Dec. 11, with prosecutors expected to file their recommendation days before the hearing. Kwon’s legal saga began after months on the run following the Terra crash, before he was arrested in Montenegro for traveling with falsified documents. He served four months there before being extradited to the United States.

Kwon’s case now sits alongside other major crypto criminal prosecutions — including that of former FTX CEO Sam Bankman-Fried, who is currently appealing his 25-year U.S. sentence.

data.title: Dragonfly Partner: Why are new public blockchains met with criticism as soon as they are launched?
data.description:
Author: Haseeb, Managing Partner of Dragonfly; Translator: @Jinse Finance xz

I used to warn entrepreneurs: the feedback you get after your project launches won't be hatred, but indifference. By default, nobody cares about your new public chain.

But now I have to stop saying that. Monad just launched this week, and I've never seen a newly launched blockchain suffer so much hostility. I've been investing in the crypto space for over seven years. Before 2023, almost every new public chain I saw received either enthusiastic or indifferent feedback. But now, new public chains are accompanied by a barrage of criticism from the moment they're born. Projects like Monad, Tempo, and MegaETH even faced a lot of verbal attacks before their mainnet launch—this is indeed a new phenomenon.

I've been trying to understand: why is this happening now? What market psychology does it reveal?

I've been trying to understand: why is this happening now? What market psychology does it reveal?

...

1、The Cure is Worse Than the Disease

Disclaimer: This will be the least metrics-heavy blockchain valuation article you've ever read. I'm not trying to sell you any fancy metrics or charts. Instead, I'm going to refute the prevailing sentiment in the crypto Twitter world—one I've been on the opposite side of for the past few years.

Back in 2024, I felt like I was fighting against financial nihilism. This view held that all assets were irrelevant, ultimately just memes, and everything we built was essentially worthless.

Thankfully, that atmosphere has dissipated. We broke that spell.

But the prevailing sentiment now I call financial cynicism: Okay, maybe some things do have value, maybe not all of them are memes, but they're severely overvalued, and it's only a matter of time before Wall Street discovers the truth.

Not all public blockchains are worthless, but they might only be worth 1/5 to 1/10 of their current trading price (have you ever seen those P/E ratios?), so you'd better pray Wall Street doesn't expose our bluff, or everything will be for naught. Many bullish analysts are now trying to counter this sentiment by constructing optimistic layer-one network valuation models, inflating P/E ratios, gross margins, and discounted cash flow. Late last year, Solana proudly adopted REV as the metric that would finally justify its valuation. They proudly announced: We—and only we—are no longer bluffing Wall Street! However, almost immediately after REV was adopted, it plummeted (though it's worth noting that SOL outperformed REV).

It's not that the REV metric itself is problematic. It is indeed a very clever metric. But the focus of this article is not on metric selection.

Then Hyperliquid appeared. This decentralized exchange has real revenue, a buyback mechanism, and a P/E ratio. So everyone exclaimed—see, we told you so! Finally, the first token with real profits and a reasonable P/E ratio has appeared (don't mention BNB, we won't discuss it). Hyperliquid will devour everything because it's obvious that Ethereum and Solana aren't making money, and we no longer need to pretend they have valuations.

...

Hyperliquid, Pump, and Sky, these tokens that emphasize buybacks, are all excellent. But the market has always had a channel to invest in exchanges—you can always buy Coinbase stock, BNB, or other assets. We hold HYPE tokens and acknowledge that it's an excellent product.

But that's not the reason people invest in ETH and SOL. The fact that a single-layer network doesn't offer exchange-level profit margins is not their motivation for buying—if they were looking for profit, they could have bought Coinbase stock.

If you think this article isn't a critique of blockchain financial metrics, you might guess I'm condemning the original sin of the token industry complex.

Clearly, everyone has lost money on tokens over the past year, including venture capitalists. Altcoins have performed poorly this year. Therefore, the other half of the mainstream voice on crypto Twitter is debating who to blame—who got greedy? Was it the greed of venture capitalists? Wintermute? Binance? Farmers?

Or is it the founders' greed? The answer remains the same: everyone is greedy. Venture capitalists, Wintertermute, liquidity miners, Binance, KOLs, and even yourself—no one is exempt. But that doesn't matter, because a properly functioning market never requires anyone to act against their own interests. If our assessment of the crypto industry is correct, investments can still succeed even if everyone is greedy. Trying to analyze a declining market by identifying "greedy individuals" is futile, akin to conducting a witch trial. I dare say no one will suddenly become greedy in 2025. Therefore, this is not the topic of this article. Many people expect me to write an article arguing why MON should be valued at X or MEGA at Y. But I don't want to write such an article, nor do I advise you to buy any particular asset. In fact, if you don't believe in them, you probably shouldn't invest at all. Will the new chain challenge succeed? Nobody knows. However, if there is indeed a possibility of success, the market will price it accordingly. If Ethereum is worth $300 billion or Solana is worth $80 billion, then a project with a 1%-5% probability of becoming the next Ethereum or Solana will have its price reflect that expected probability. What's puzzling is that the crypto Twitter community is outraged by this, but it's no different from the biotechnology field. A drug with less than a 10% chance of curing Alzheimer's disease, even if there's a 90% chance it won't pass Phase III clinical trials and will become worthless, will still be valued at billions of dollars by the market. This is a manifestation of mathematical logic—the market is, as it turns out, very good at calculating. Binary results are based on probability pricing, not growth rates or moral flaws. This belongs to the valuation philosophy of the "shut up and calculate" school. I think discussing this issue is pointless. "5% chance of success? Impossible, that's clearly 10%!" For any individual token, the market, not the article, is the best evaluator. Therefore, what this article truly explores is: the crypto Twitter community seems to have lost faith in the value of public blockchains. I believe this isn't because they question the ability of new public blockchains to gain market share—we just witnessed Solana rise from the ashes and dominate the market in less than two years. This, while not easy, is certainly possible. The deeper reason lies in the growing belief that even if a new public blockchain wins, there's no worthwhile prize to fight for. If ETH is just a meme and will never generate real revenue, then even if you win, you won't achieve a $300 billion valuation. This race isn't worth participating in because all valuations are bubbles that will burst before you claim your reward. Optimism about public blockchain valuations seems out of place. It's not that no one is optimistic—obviously, there are bullish voices in the market. Where there's a seller, there's a buyer. Despite the crypto Twitter influencers' enthusiasm for predicting the downfall of layer one networks, some are still confidently buying SOL at $140 and ETH at $3,000. However, a prevailing belief is that the smartest people have already stopped buying smart contract public chains. The smart people know the trick has been exposed, either now or soon. Those buying now are just suckers—ride-hailing drivers, people like Tom Lee, and KOLs who constantly talk about "trillion-dollar market capitalization." Perhaps the US Treasury. But definitely not smart money. This is pure nonsense. I don't believe it, and you shouldn't either. Therefore, I feel it's necessary to write a "Manifesto of the Smart," explaining the value of general-purpose public chains. This article is not about Monad or MegaETH; it's actually a defense of ETH and SOL. Because if you accept the value of ETH and SOL, the rest of the reasoning falls into place.

Defending the valuations of ETH and SOL is not my job as a venture capitalist, but since no one else is willing to do it, I'll take the plunge.

2, Perceiving Exponential Growth

My partner, Bo, experienced the Chinese internet boom firsthand as a venture capitalist. Although I've become numb to the notion that "the crypto industry is like the internet," his stories always remind me of the high cost of misjudging trends.

He often tells a story: In the early 21st century, when all the early e-commerce venture capitalists (at the time, it was just a small circle) gathered for coffee, they debated the size of the e-commerce market.

Will it mainly sell electronics (perhaps only geeks use computers)? Can it attract female users (perhaps they value tactile experience more)? What about fresh food (perishable goods might be unmanageable)?

For early-stage venture capitalists, the answers to these questions directly determine investment direction and valuation logic. The result, of course, proved them all wildly wrong. E-commerce would sell everything, targeting customers worldwide. But at the time, nobody truly believed it, and even those who did dared not voice it because it seemed so absurd. Only by waiting long enough would exponential growth materialize. Even among those who believed in e-commerce at the time, very few thought it would reach its later scale. And those few who remained steadfast and held on became almost all billionaires. Other venture capitalists—who, according to Bo, were among them—sold out prematurely. Today, in the crypto space, believing in exponential growth is outdated. But I believe in the exponential law of the crypto world. Because I've experienced it firsthand. When I first entered the industry, nobody used these technologies: they were small-scale, riddled with bugs, and offered a terrible user experience. The total value locked on-chain is only a few million dollars. When we invested in first-generation DeFi projects—MakerDAO, Compound, and 1inch—they were still experimental products. I used EtherDelta, which had a daily trading volume of only a few million dollars, and that was considered a huge success at the time. Today, the daily trading volume on-chain routinely exceeds tens of billions of dollars. I used to think it was absurd that Tether's issuance exceeded 1 billion and it was reported by the New York Times as a Ponzi scheme on the verge of collapse; now stablecoins have a market capitalization of over $300 billion and are regulated by the Federal Reserve. I believe in exponential growth because I have witnessed it time and time again. But you might argue: stablecoin growth may be exponential, and so may DeFi trading volume, but the value hasn't been deposited into ETH or SOL. Public chains have failed to capture this value. To that, my answer is: you still don't believe in the exponential law. Because the answer to exponential growth remains the same: it doesn't matter. The future scale will far exceed today's imagination; when the great changes arrive, economies of scale will compensate for everything. See the image below. This is Amazon's profit and loss statement from 1995 to 2019, spanning 24 years. The red line represents revenue, and the gray line represents profit. See that small upward curve at the end of the gray line? That's when Amazon truly began to turn a profit in its 22nd year. When this gray line, representing net profit, first moved above zero, Amazon had already been established for 22 years. Every year prior, columnists, critics, and short sellers claimed Amazon was a Ponzi scheme that would never be profitable. Ethereum, meanwhile, had just turned 10. Here's how Amazon's stock performed over its first decade: A decade of turmoil. During this time, Amazon was constantly surrounded by skeptics and critics: Was e-commerce just a venture-funded charity? Who cared if they dumped cheap, low-quality goods on bargain hunters? How could they possibly make money like Walmart or General Electric? If you were arguing about Amazon's P/E ratio back then, you chose the wrong paradigm—it was the linear growth paradigm. But e-commerce isn't a linear trend, so everyone arguing about P/E ratios for the past 22 years has been wildly wrong. No matter how much you bid, no matter when you buy, your bullishness is far from sufficient. Because that's the law of exponential growth. For truly exponential technology, no matter how large you think it can reach, it will always exceed your imagination. This is precisely what Silicon Valley has always understood better than Wall Street. Silicon Valley grew through exponential growth, while Wall Street is steeped in linear thinking. In the past few years, the center of gravity of the crypto world has shifted from Silicon Valley to Wall Street—you can feel the change. Admittedly, crypto growth isn't as smooth as e-commerce growth. It's more explosive, with intermittent leaps. This is because crypto involves currency, is deeply tied to macro forces, and faces a more intense regulatory tug-of-war than e-commerce. Encryption strikes at the heart of a nation's economy—currency—making it more unsettling to governments than e-commerce. But the inevitability of exponential growth remains undiminished. This is a crude argument, but if encryption truly represents exponential growth, then this crude argument is correct. Zooming out, financial assets crave freedom. They crave openness. They crave interconnectivity. Encryption transforms financial assets into file formats, making sending dollars or stocks as simple as sending a PDF. It allows everything to communicate with each other, creating a 24/7, global, interconnected open ecosystem. Openness will ultimately prevail. This is a core principle I learned from the internet age. The vested interests will resist, governments will bluff, but ultimately they will succumb to the ubiquity, creativity, and extreme efficiency this technology brings. Just as the internet reshaped all industries, blockchain will similarly devour the entire financial and monetary system. Yes—given enough time—it will devour everything. There's an old saying: people always overestimate what will change in two years and underestimate what will change in ten. If you believe in exponential growth, if you broaden your perspective sufficiently, everything will still seem cheap. Every day, holders outlast sellers and detractors—this should keep you humble. Big capital has a much longer time horizon than the swing traders on crypto Twitter might convince you of. History has taught big capital not to fight against major technological changes. Remember that grand story that initially lured you into buying ETH or SOL? Big capital still believes in that story, and it never stops. So, what is my core argument? I argue that applying price-to-earnings ratios to smart contract public chains (the so-called "revenue meta-narrative") is essentially abandoning exponential growth. This means you've relegated the industry to a linear growth paradigm, that you believe 30 million daily active users and less than 1% of the total M2 money supply is the end—the crypto world is just one of countless industries, a marginal player. It hasn't won, nor is it inevitable. More importantly, I urge people to become its believers. Not just believers, but long-term believers. I firmly believe this exponential revolution will surpass any career you've ever been involved in. This is your "e-commerce revolution." When you're old and look back, you'll tell your children and grandchildren—I witnessed this upheaval. Not everyone believed that social systems could be restructured, or that programs running on our collectively owned decentralized computers could fundamentally change money and finance. But it did happen. It changed the world. And you were a part of it.
